Atlanta Restaurant Openings
Kinship Butcher & Sundry Team to Open New Restaurant
So. Fox will open next door to Kinship in Virginia-Highland this spring
by
Henna Bakshi
Jan 29, 2026, 5:08 PM UTC
Facebook
Link
The rendering for So. Fox shows light, wooden tables with ample light flooding in from the windows.
So. Fox
Henna Bakshi
is the Regional and Audience Editor at Eater and an award-winning food and wine journalist with a WSET (Wine and Spirits Education Trust) Level 3 degree. She oversees coverage in Atlanta, Chicago, Seattle, Portland, Nashville, and the Carolinas.
Can’t stop, won’t stop chef Myles Moody and sommelier Rachael Pack. The duo behind Kinship Butcher & Sundry is in major expansion mode: with the upcoming opening of their
new location of Kinship in Grant Park
, they are now gearing up to open a new restaurant,
So. Fox
, this spring. They are doing this all while hosting their
sold-out dinner pop-ups, k|n
.
So. Fox (short for Southern Fox — another name for the Muscadine or Scuppernong grape) will open directly next door to Kinship in Virginia-Highland. It will feature a seasonal menu by Moody, and a natural wine program by Pack. The couple will tap into their fine dining experiences from two-Michelin-starred
Aska
(where the two met),
Eleven Madison Park
, and
Blue Hill at Stone Barns
, with a focus on proteins and produce sourced from local farms and small purveyors.
Rendering shows lightly-colored tables and ample sunlight flooding the room.
So. Fox
Related
A Candlelit Butcher Shop Is Hosting Atlanta’s Most Intimate Meal
If the dinner pop-up  k|n is any indication of the food, diners can expect a lot of intricate fermentation techniques, gorgeous cuts of beef and fish, and thoughtful organic and biodynamic wine and non-alcoholic pairings. So. Fox will seat 50 people, with renderings showing ample sunlight flooding a light wood-toned dining room. The building itself has historic significance to Atlanta.
So. Fox will open in a building that was originally a Standard Oil filling station almost 100 years ago.
Archive photo, 1930
“This main corner lot has been serving the Virginia-Highland community in a variety of ways for 100 years,” says building owner Lynn Dewitt in a statement. “It was originally a Standard Oil filling station and then expanded with Herrington Service Station for decades.”
The building was then converted to house Farm Burger, where So. Fox will go now. It will open in the coming months, and the second location of Kinship with an expanded seafood butchery will open this fall.

From mortadella-stacked sandwiches with burrata to sweet and spicy barbecue

,

One of the best parts of a workday is breaking for lunch. These restaurants hit all the right notes, from
power lunch hot spots
for a quick meeting to places with a
great deal
. Here are the best places for a midday treat in Atlanta.
This latest update by
Henna Bakshi
includes new additions to the list, like Spring 2nd Branch for Korean fare, Necessary Purveyor for mortadella sandwiches, and Tio Lucho’s for lunch ceviche, among others. The restaurants are listed in geographic order from north to south. Don’t see a favorite lunch spot listed? Send Eater

Atlanta Restaurant News
A Candlelit Butcher Shop Is Hosting Atlanta’s Most Intimate Meal
It has only four seats
by
Henna Bakshi
Aug 4, 2025, 4:45 PM UTC
Facebook
Link
This September, Kinship Butcher and Sundry is transforming into a four-seat fine-dining destination
|  Dave Crawford
Henna Bakshi
is the Regional and Audience Editor at Eater and an award-winning food and wine journalist with a WSET (Wine and Spirits Education Trust) Level 3 degree. She oversees coverage in Atlanta, Chicago, Seattle, Portland, Nashville, and the Carolinas.
Welcome to
another installment
of
Scene Report in Atlanta
, a new column in which Eater captures the vibe of a notable Atlanta restaurant at a specific moment in time.
What is usually a bustling, sun-lit cafe and butchery, with people lining up for its
famous breakfast sandwich
on brioche, transforms into a candlelit oasis with four seats lining the bar. After hours at 8:30 p.m.,
Kinship Butcher and Sundry
co-owner Rachael Pack greets you at the door, while her husband, Myles Moody, is a buzzing shadow with pots and pans behind the meat counter. The produce light is on with fairytale eggplants and peppers on display, the cheese case beckons, and folded napkins and freshly polished wine glasses wait dramatically at the handmade bar. And that’s how dinner begins.
The produce section at Kinship is dimly lit during dinner.
Andy Leverett
Starting in September, Kinship in Virginia-Highlands will debut a
12-course tasting menu
, seating just four guests per night, two weekends a month.
Priced at $325
, the hyper-intimate dinner showcases local ingredients and wine pairings from chef Moody (formerly of
Eleven Madison Park
,
Aska
, and
Blue Hill at Stone Barns
) and sommelier Pack (also formerly of Aska, where the couple met). Eater got an exclusive first look at dinner. Here’s what to expect.
The food
Most ingredients rely on what’s fresh and new, but here, dishes embrace aging and fermentation. Aged and preserved fruits appear on both plate and in glass, balancing summer garden brightness with the depth of time.
The first course, aptly named the Garden, is a refreshing herb bundle steeped in a chilled, foaming cucumber drink alongside a lettuce, cheese, and preserved chamomile tartelet. (The ethos is immediately very EMP and
Noma
.) The second is a fermented green tomato with capered coriander seeds — a revelation that coriander seeds can be salted and pickled like capers, offering tiny bursts of acid and spice.
